content: Welcome to the Small Claims Court of Illinois; I have reviewed your claim regarding the dental injury sustained while consuming a chicken dish. Please provide the itemized restaurant receipt from May 30th, the dental X-rays and treatment records showing the fracture and subsequent implant, and all paid invoices or receipts totaling the $4,500 medical cost.

Good morning your honor. I have the documents right here. This is the dental invoice for the extracion totaling $4500, and here  is the receipt and statement confirming my visit to central east asian cuisine on may 30th, 2024.

While we regret the plaintiff's injury, the 'Big Plate Whole Chicken' is a traditional dish where bone-in components are an inherent and expected feature, meaning the diner assumes the risk of bones. Furthermore, the provided dental documentation appears inconsistent and lacks the professional clarity required to prove our meal was the proximate cause of the dental damage.

I have reviewed the restaurant receipt and dental invoice provided in turn_1_1_evidence_1.png. Please provide the dental X-rays and the specific clinical notes from your dentist that document the fracture immediately following the May 30th incident. Under Illinois law, the court must determine whether a consumer would reasonably expect to encounter such a bone in this specific dish.
I don't have the x ray, however, I have dentist report

Your Honor, the plaintiff's submitted dental invoice contains nonsensical text and suspicious formatting, and they have failed to produce the actual X-rays as ordered by this court. Furthermore, since 'Big Plate Whole Chicken' is a bone-in dish, the presence of bone fragments is a natural and reasonably expected feature, meaning the restaurant is not liable for an inherent risk the diner assumes.

While the clinical report in turn_4_1_evidence_3.png provides a professional medical assessment of the injury, the dental invoice provided in turn_1_1_evidence_1.png contains illegible text and inconsistent figures that prevent the court from verifying your actual expenses. Please provide a clear, itemized billing statement or insurance Explanation of Benefits from the dental office that legibly lists the costs for the extraction and implant. Additionally, be prepared to discuss why a consumer should not reasonably expect to encounter bone fragments in a dish titled 'Whole Chicken.'
This is the only one I have

The plaintiff has failed to provide the legible, itemized billing or radiographic evidence requested by this court, relying instead on a document containing nonsensical text and inconsistent figures. Furthermore, because bones are a natural and inherent component of a dish titled 'Whole Chicken,' the plaintiff assumed the risk of injury under the reasonable expectation test.

The court finds in favor of the defendant and denies the plaintiff's claim for $5,500. Under Illinois law, the 'reasonable expectation' test applies, and a consumer should reasonably expect to encounter bones in a dish explicitly titled 'Whole Chicken.' Furthermore, the plaintiff failed to provide a legible, authentic itemized bill to substantiate the claimed damages, as the document in turn_1_1_evidence_1.png contains nonsensical text and unreliable figures.
